text: SIFMA recommended a full close of the U.S. dollar-denominated fixed income market on Friday, June 19, 2026 for Juneteenth, and U.S. equity cash markets were closed, so there was no live U.S. Treasury or Wall Street session and no June 19 Treasury par-yield print; the day's macro signal therefore came from global FX, ICE Brent and European cash rates.

calculation.method: read the official Treasury par-yield CSV; 10Y minus 2Y for the last available print; confirmed no 06/19/2026 row exists

text: With the U.S. fixed income market closed for Juneteenth, the official Treasury par-yield CSV showed no June 19, 2026 row; the last print remained June 18 at 2-year 4.19%, 10-year 4.46% and 30-year 4.90%, a +27 bp 2s10s spread (versus +29 bp on June 17), so U.S. rates were effectively frozen at Thursday's close through the holiday.

value.weekly_context: strongest weekly gain in about a month, mostly at the yen's expense

calculation.method: vendor close vs prior session; intraday high cross-checked against the CNBC 52-week-high stamp dated 06/19/26 and the Reuters 13-month-high description

text: The U.S. Dollar Index ended Friday, June 19, 2026 little changed near 100.80 (about -0.05%) after printing a fresh 13-month/52-week intraday high of 101.13 (low 100.70) versus the June 18 close of 100.849; FX traded through the U.S. cash holiday and the dollar logged its strongest weekly gain in roughly a month, mostly at the yen's expense.

value.vendor_note: Trading Economics 2.96% (+4 bp) vs WSJ 2.992% (+6 bp); roughly 3 bp vendor spread disclosed

calculation.method: vendor 10Y Bund yield vs prior session; Trading Economics cross-checked against WSJ; Bundesbank daily-yield table cited as official primary

text: Germany's 10-year Bund yield rose on June 19, 2026 to roughly 2.96%-2.99% - Trading Economics dated it 2.96% (+4 bp) and the Wall Street Journal 2.992% (+6 bp), about a 3 bp vendor spread - extending a modest move higher into the European close while U.S. rates were frozen by the holiday; the Deutsche Bundesbank daily-yield table for 19.06.2026 is the official primary reference.

value.vendor_note: FT 4.84 (+0.003, ~flat) vs WSJ 4.846% (+0.078); level agrees, daily-change basis differs

calculation.method: vendor 10Y gilt yield; FT end-of-day 18:30 BST tearsheet cross-checked against WSJ

text: The UK 10-year gilt yield was about 4.84% at the London close on June 19, 2026 (FT 4.84, +0.003 pp at 18:30 BST; WSJ 4.846%, +0.078 pp) - broadly steady at an elevated level with the gilt market open through the U.S. Juneteenth holiday, the vendor disagreement confined to the size of the daily change rather than the level.

subject.name: Germany producer price index, industrial products (domestic sales)

value.release: Destatis press release No. 212 of 19 June 2026

calculation.method: official Destatis y/y and m/m producer-price change for May 2026; breakdown reconciled with Trading Economics

text: Germany's producer prices for industrial products rose 2.2% year on year and 0.3% on the month in May 2026, Destatis reported on June 19, 2026 (press release No. 212) - the fastest annual pace since May 2023 but below the 2.5% y/y and 0.7% m/m forecasts. The rise was led by intermediate goods (+4.2%) and energy (+2.5% y/y), with mineral-oil products up 34.9% y/y on the Iran/Middle East conflict (naphtha +60.9%, heating oil +52.8%, motor fuels +24.2%); excluding energy, producer prices rose 2.3% y/y, while non-durable consumer goods fell 1.7%.

value.weekly_context: sharp week-long selloff tied to the U.S.-Iran deal reopening the Strait of Hormuz; oil dipped below $80 intraday then erased losses after the Iran talks were called off
value.us_market_note: NYMEX WTI traded only on CME's shortened Juneteenth session

calculation.method: vendor front-month Brent close vs prior session; Yahoo BZ=F cross-checked against Trading Economics

text: Brent crude rebounded on Friday, June 19, 2026, ending near $80.59/bbl, up about $0.74 or 0.93% (range $78.80-$80.82; Trading Economics cross-checks $80.58) after dipping below $80 intraday and erasing earlier losses when U.S. Vice President JD Vance pulled out of Friday's Iran talks. The bounce capped a sharp week-long selloff driven by the U.S.-Iran deal reopening the Strait of Hormuz, while U.S. WTI traded only on CME's shortened Juneteenth session.

value.us_iran: U.S. VP JD Vance pulled out of planned Friday talks with Iranian negotiators in Switzerland
value.lebanon: Israel and Hezbollah agreed a ceasefire in Lebanon, per a U.S. official
value.hormuz: tankers began transiting the Strait of Hormuz after the U.S. lifted its blockade on Iran on Thursday
value.yen: near a 40-year low, weakest since late 1986, down 5 of the last 6 weeks; Tokyo intervention warnings

text: The June 19, 2026 macro session was set by geopolitics and FX rather than U.S. data, with U.S. cash markets shut for Juneteenth. Reuters reported global shares dipped (MSCI All-World -0.15%, STOXX Europe -0.12%, U.S. futures -0.1% to -0.2%) after VP JD Vance pulled out of Friday peace talks with Iran in Switzerland, even as Israel and Hezbollah agreed a Lebanon ceasefire and tankers resumed transit through the Strait of Hormuz following Thursday's lifting of the U.S. blockade; the yen sat near a 40-year low (weakest since late 1986) on a five-of-six-week slide, drawing intervention warnings from Tokyo.
